title: German chocolate cake
subtitle: A three-layer chocolate cake with coconut-pecan filling
excerpt: The American classic: three layers of a mild chocolate sponge alternating with a rich coconut and pecan caramel filling. Not a German recipe — named after Sam German, who developed the sweet baking chocolate used in the original 1957 recipe.
sourceNotes: German chocolate cake takes its name from Sam German, a baker who developed a mild sweet chocolate for Baker's Chocolate Company in the 1850s. The cake recipe using this chocolate was published in a Dallas Morning Star recipe column in 1957 by a home baker, and quickly became a national phenomenon. The recipe was published publicly through the newspaper and is now in the public domain. The formula here follows the original approach: a mild chocolate butter cake with the distinctive coconut-pecan caramel frosting.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted layers wrapped tightly. The coconut-pecan filling does not freeze well.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The coconut-pecan filling can be made 2 days ahead and refrigerated. Bring to room temperature before spreading.

glossaryTerms.definition: Unsweetened canned milk with about 60% of the water removed. Richer than regular milk and with a slightly caramelised flavour. In the coconut-pecan filling it acts as the liquid for the egg-yolk custard base.

content.text: German chocolate cake is less intensely chocolatey than most chocolate layer cakes — the original recipe used a mild, sweetened chocolate. That mildness is right for this recipe; the coconut-pecan filling is the dominant flavour and a very dark chocolate would fight it. The filling uses
cooked with egg yolks and butter into a thick caramel-style custard.

Combine the evaporated milk, sugar, beaten egg yolks, and butter in a saucepan. Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, for 12 to 15 minutes until the mixture thickens and turns a light amber-caramel colour. Remove from heat and stir in the vanilla, coconut, and pecans. The filling will thicken further as it cools; allow it to reach room temperature before using.

Preheat the oven to 175°C conventional. Grease and line three 23 cm round tins. Whisk together the dry ingredients. In a separate bowl, whisk the eggs, buttermilk, oil, and vanilla. Combine wet and dry ingredients, then add the hot water in a steady stream and stir until smooth — the batter will be thin. Divide between the tins and bake for 30 to 35 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tins for 15 minutes, then on racks.

Place the first sponge layer on a plate. Spread one third of the cooled filling over the top, spreading to within 1 cm of the edge. Add the second layer and repeat, then place the third layer on top and finish with the remaining filling. The sides of this cake are traditionally left unfrosted; the coconut-pecan filling is the only topping required.
